questions.question: Early scientific opinion treated sleep as a passive state of reduced brain activity.
questions.explanation: The passage says earlier consensus held that sleep was a passive state of reduced brain activity.

questions.question: REM sleep is linked to emotional regulation and memory consolidation.
questions.explanation: The passage states that REM sleep appears critical for emotional regulation and memory consolidation.

questions.question: Chronic sleep deprivation means regularly sleeping fewer than six hours a night.
questions.explanation: The passage defines chronic sleep deprivation as consistently sleeping fewer than seven hours per night.

questions.question: GDP includes unpaid domestic labour in its calculation of national wellbeing.
questions.explanation: The passage says GDP does not account for unpaid domestic labour.

questions.question: New Zealand introduced a Wellbeing Budget in 2019.
questions.explanation: The passage states that New Zealand adopted a Wellbeing Budget in 2019.

questions.question: Wellbeing metrics are easy to measure consistently across countries.
questions.explanation: The passage says critics argue wellbeing metrics are inherently subjective and difficult to measure consistently.

questions.question: Around 380 million tonnes of plastic are produced globally each year.
questions.explanation: The passage gives that figure directly.

questions.question: Only about nine percent of all plastic ever made has been recycled.
questions.explanation: The passage says only nine percent of all plastic ever manufactured has been recycled.

questions.question: Microplastics have been detected in human blood.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly says microplastics have been detected in human blood.

questions.question: Remote work became mainstream during the COVID-19 pandemic.
questions.explanation: The passage says remote work became mainstream almost overnight during the pandemic.

questions.question: Some economists think fully remote work could reduce innovation.
questions.explanation: The passage states that some economists argue fully remote work may reduce innovation.

questions.question: French loanwords became socially more prestigious than Old English equivalents in some domains.
questions.explanation: The passage says French-derived words often carried higher social status than Old English equivalents.

questions.question: The Oxford English Dictionary tracks over 60,000 entries.
questions.explanation: The passage says it tracks over 600,000 entries, not 60,000.

questions.question:
Choose the correct sentence.

The number of applicants ____ increased this year.
questions.explanation: 'The number of' is singular, so the verb takes 'has.'

questions.question: Which noun is uncountable?
questions.explanation: Feedback is uncountable, so it is not pluralised.

questions.question:
Choose the best collocation.

Students should ____ a conclusion from the evidence.
questions.explanation: The natural collocation is 'reach a conclusion.'

questions.question: In IELTS Writing Task 1, the overview should mainly:
options: summarise the key trends and comparisons
repeat the introduction word for word
questions.answer: summarise the key trends and comparisons
questions.explanation: Task 1 overview paragraphs should describe the main patterns, not every detail.

questions.question: A strong IELTS Writing Task 2 introduction should:
options: answer the question directly and state the writer's position
repeat the topic three times for emphasis
include detailed examples from the body paragraphs
avoid mentioning the question at all
questions.answer: answer the question directly and state the writer's position
questions.explanation: The introduction should clearly frame the essay's position and task response.

questions.question: In a listening task, if a speaker gives one answer and immediately corrects it, you should write:
options: both versions separated by a slash
questions.explanation: The corrected statement is the answer; the first version is usually the distractor.

questions.question: IELTS Reading has:
options: only one chance to revise each answer
questions.explanation: Guessing is better than leaving an answer blank because wrong answers are not penalised.

questions.question:
Choose the word that completes the phrase.

The new policy will ____ into effect next month.
questions.explanation: The fixed phrase is 'come into effect.'

questions.question:
Choose the correct form of the word in brackets.

The committee reached a highly ____ decision. (significant)
questions.explanation: The gap needs an adjective to modify 'decision.'

questions.question:
Complete the sentence using the key word.

He was too tired to continue.

He was so tired that he ____ continue.
questions.explanation: The meaning is preserved with 'so tired that he couldn't continue.'

questions.question:
Choose the best phrase.

The proposal was met with widespread ____.
questions.explanation: The noun 'approval' fits the sentence naturally.

questions.question: CELPIP Speaking is scored by:
questions.explanation: CELPIP speaking is computer-delivered and AI-scored.

questions.question: In CELPIP Reading, Part 4 is generally the most demanding section.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that Part 4 (Viewpoints) is the most demanding.

questions.question: In CELPIP Writing Task 1, the tone should match:
options: a presentation to a large audience
questions.explanation: CELPIP email tone changes depending on who you are writing to.

questions.question: CELPIP and IELTS both use the same speaking test format.
questions.explanation: CELPIP speaking is computer-delivered and AI-scored, while IELTS speaking is face-to-face.

questions.question: Urban farming has grown significantly over the last twenty years.
questions.explanation: 'Expanded rapidly over the past two decades' directly confirms this. TRUE.

questions.question: Urban farming consistently produces more food per square metre than conventional agriculture.
questions.explanation: The passage states yields per square metre are 'typically lower than in conventional agriculture.' FALSE.

questions.question: Low-income communities face financial barriers when trying to establish urban farms.
questions.explanation: 'The cost of establishing rooftop or vertical farms is often prohibitive for low-income communities.' TRUE.

questions.question: The University of Sheffield study was the first to examine urban farming's caloric potential.
questions.explanation: The passage cites the study's findings but says nothing about whether it was the first of its kind. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: Converting all suitable urban surfaces to food production would meet roughly two percent of UK caloric needs.
questions.explanation: 'Total caloric output would cover only about two percent of national demand.' TRUE.

questions.question: Governments have introduced financial incentives to support urban farming initiatives.
questions.explanation: Government incentives are not mentioned anywhere in the passage. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: Monarch butterflies can travel up to 4,500 kilometres during migration.
questions.explanation: The passage states explicitly 'travel up to 4,500 kilometres.' TRUE.

questions.question: The butterflies that migrate south in autumn are the same individuals that flew north in spring.
questions.explanation: 'The butterflies that make the southward journey are not the same individuals' — they are great-grandchildren. FALSE.

questions.question: Scientists have fully explained how monarchs navigate to ancestral forests.
questions.explanation: 'A phenomenon researchers have yet to fully explain' — the opposite is stated. FALSE.

questions.question: Monarch populations have declined by more than 80 percent since the 1990s.
questions.explanation: Directly stated in the final sentence of the passage. TRUE.

questions.question: Conservation programmes have begun to reverse the decline in monarch populations.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions decline but says nothing about conservation programmes or reversal. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: Monarchs use both the sun and an internal clock to navigate.
questions.explanation: 'A combination of the sun's position and an internal circadian clock.' TRUE.

questions.question: Fleming discovered penicillin's antibacterial properties in 1928.
questions.explanation: Directly stated: 'Alexander Fleming's observation of penicillin's antibacterial properties in 1928.' TRUE.

questions.question: Antimicrobial resistance has been caused solely by overuse in human medicine.
questions.explanation: The passage attributes AMR to both human medicine AND livestock farming — not solely human medicine. FALSE.

questions.question: The WHO ranks AMR as a greater threat than climate change.
questions.explanation: The WHO calls AMR 'one of the greatest threats' but no comparison with climate change is made. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: Some diseases have developed strains that are nearly impossible to treat.
questions.explanation: 'Some strains...have become virtually untreatable.' TRUE.

questions.question: Without global action, routine surgeries could become life-threatening by 2050.
questions.explanation: Directly stated in the final sentence of the passage. TRUE.

questions.question: Earlier scientific thinking viewed sleep as a state of reduced brain activity.
questions.explanation: 'Earlier scientific consensus held that sleep was a passive state of reduced brain activity.' TRUE.

questions.question: REM sleep is linked to memory consolidation and emotional regulation.
questions.explanation: 'REM sleep...appears critical for emotional regulation and memory consolidation.' TRUE.

questions.question: Slow-wave sleep is the lightest stage of the sleep cycle.
questions.explanation: The passage calls slow-wave sleep 'the deepest stage' — the opposite of lightest. FALSE.

questions.question: Governments in developed countries have introduced policies to improve adult sleep habits.
questions.explanation: Government policy is never mentioned. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: Sleeping fewer than seven hours regularly is associated with cardiovascular disease risk.
questions.explanation: 'Increased risk of cardiovascular disease' is explicitly listed among consequences of chronic deprivation. TRUE.

questions.question: GDP measures the total monetary value of goods and services produced in a country.
questions.explanation: This is the definition given in the passage. TRUE.

questions.question: GDP accounts for unpaid domestic labour when calculating national output.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly lists unpaid domestic labour as something GDP does NOT account for. FALSE.

questions.question: New Zealand adopted a Wellbeing Budget in 2019.
questions.explanation: Directly stated in the passage. TRUE.

questions.question: Most economists support wellbeing metrics as a primary guide for fiscal policy.
questions.explanation: Critics are mentioned, but the passage gives no information about what most economists believe overall. NOT GIVEN.

questions.question: A country experiencing GDP growth cannot simultaneously have rising poverty.
questions.explanation: The passage states explicitly: 'A country can register GDP growth while simultaneously experiencing rising poverty.' FALSE.

questions.question: What percentage of all plastic ever manufactured has been recycled?
questions.explanation: 'Only nine percent of all plastic ever manufactured has been recycled.' Direct retrieval. B.

questions.question: What comparison does the passage use to convey the size of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch?
options: Three times the size of France
Larger than the entire Pacific Ocean
The size of the United Kingdom
questions.answer: Three times the size of France
questions.explanation: 'Covers an area roughly three times the size of France.' Direct retrieval. A.

questions.question: Where have microplastics been detected, according to the passage?
options: In ocean trenches, Arctic ice, and human blood
In the atmosphere above major cities
questions.answer: In ocean trenches, Arctic ice, and human blood
questions.explanation: All three locations are explicitly listed in the passage. B.

questions.question: What is the passage's main argument about single-use plastic bans?
options: They are the most effective solution available
They are unnecessary given market forces
They are insufficient without fundamental production and consumption changes
They have successfully reduced ocean plastic by 50 percent
questions.answer: They are insufficient without fundamental production and consumption changes
questions.explanation: 'Regulatory measures alone are insufficient without fundamental changes to production and consumption patterns.' C.

questions.question: According to the passage, which group was particularly affected by reduced mentorship opportunities during remote work?
questions.explanation: 'Reduced opportunities for mentorship and career progression, particularly among younger employees.' B.

questions.question: What benefit for employers does the passage specifically mention?
options: Access to a wider talent pool
questions.answer: Access to a wider talent pool
questions.explanation: 'Access to a wider talent pool' is explicitly listed as an employer benefit. B.

questions.question: Some economists argue that fully remote work may reduce innovation because:
options: Informal spontaneous interactions in shared spaces drive innovation
Home environments are too distracting for creative thinking
questions.answer: Informal spontaneous interactions in shared spaces drive innovation
questions.explanation: 'Innovation, which has historically been driven by informal, spontaneous interactions in shared physical spaces.' B.

questions.question: Which event introduced significant French vocabulary into English?
questions.explanation: 'The Norman Conquest of 1066 introduced a vast French vocabulary.' B.

questions.question: What linguistic effect did French vocabulary have on English social structure?
options: It simplified the English class system
French-derived words carried higher social status than Old English equivalents
It created a new dialect spoken only in cities
questions.answer: French-derived words carried higher social status than Old English equivalents
questions.explanation: 'French-derived words often carried higher social status than their Old English equivalents.' B.

questions.question: Approximately how many entries does the Oxford English Dictionary track?
questions.explanation: 'Over 600,000 entries' is stated directly. C.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: The data shows a clear upward trend.
The data show a clear upward trend.
The datas shows a clear upward trend.
The data are showing a clear upward trend always.
questions.answer: The data shows a clear upward trend.
questions.explanation: 'Data' can take singular or plural agreement in modern academic English. Both A and B are technically acceptable, but 'data shows' is the more common academic convention tested in IELTS. A is the safest answer.

questions.question: Which sentence uses subject-verb agreement correctly?
options: The government have decided to raise taxes.
The committee are divided on the issue.
Neither the teacher nor the students was present.
The number of applicants has increased this year.
questions.answer: The number of applicants has increased this year.
questions.explanation: 'The number of' takes a singular verb. 'Neither...nor' agrees with the nearest noun (students = plural → were). D is the only fully correct option.

questions.question: Select the correct conditional: 'If she ___ harder, she would have passed the exam.'
questions.explanation: Third conditional (unreal past): 'If + past perfect, would have + past participle.' 'Had studied' is correct. B.

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
options: She gave me many informations about the course.
She provided me with a lot of information about the course.
She told me many informations regarding the course.
She gave many information to me about the course.
questions.answer: She provided me with a lot of information about the course.
questions.explanation: 'Information' is an uncountable noun — never plural, never 'many informations.' 'A lot of information' is correct. B.

questions.question: 'Decide' is always followed by which verb form?
questions.explanation: 'Decide' belongs to the group of verbs followed by the infinitive: decide to do, want to do, hope to do. B.

questions.question: Which sentence correctly uses a discourse marker?
questions.explanation: When a discourse marker opens a sentence, a comma must follow it immediately. 'However, + clause' is the standard form. C.

questions.question: Which sentence correctly uses reported speech?
options: She said she will submit the report tomorrow.
She said she would submit the report the next day.
questions.answer: She said she would submit the report the next day.
questions.explanation: 'Will' becomes 'would'; 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day'; 'said' needs no object (unlike 'told'). B.
